title: Bart The Fink

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Standard opening with Homer's signature exclamation
jokes.punchline: The iconic catchphrase itself
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase - perfectly integrated to character but routine by this point in series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: The only stipulation is that you spend one night in a haunted house. - Oh. Isn't that somewhat unusual? - No. It's a standard clause.
jokes.setup: Marge questions the unusual inheritance requirement
jokes.punchline: Lawyer treats haunted house clause as completely normal legal practice
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist humor with good deadpan delivery - treats supernatural as mundane bureaucracy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me the house is haunted?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Oh yes, that's standard in the property deed. Most estates have some form of supernatural activity. Nothing to worry about, Mrs. Simpson. It's all perfectly legal.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But... ghosts?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Ghosts, poltergeists, occasionally a vengeful spirit or two. It's all boilerplate stuff. I'd be more concerned if the house *wasn't* haunted. That would be unusual.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm sorry. I must have continued talking after you left the office. I do that sometimes.
jokes.setup: Lawyer explains Ann Landers getting most of the inheritance
jokes.punchline: Admits to talking to empty room out of habit
jokes.explanation: Nice character detail about lawyer's social awkwardness with good self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: I'm afraid most of the estate goes to Ann Landers.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry. Force of habit. She's been dead for fifteen years.

jokes.text: There's a special on tacos down at the Taco Mat- hundred tacos for $100- I'm gonna get that.
jokes.setup: Question about how to spend inheritance money
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately opts for buying 100 tacos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer behavior - immediate gratification with absurd quantity of food

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 100 tacos?! Why didn't you say so?!

jokes.text: Tacos? Public broadcasting? I won't have you kids throwing away your money like that.
jokes.setup: Homer wants 100 tacos, Lisa wants to support PBS
jokes.punchline: Marge equating educational TV funding with wasteful taco binges
jokes.explanation: Brilliant false equivalence that reveals Marge's practical mindset while creating absurd moral logic.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't just spend a hundred dollars on tacos! That's wasteful and irresponsible.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, a man's gotta eat!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, Dad, PBS needs our pledge to keep educational programming on the air.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Lisa, I suppose there's no difference between throwing away money on a hundred tacos and throwing it away on public television.

jokes.text: Boy, I sure could go for 100 tacos right about now.
jokes.setup: At bank instead of taco place
jokes.punchline: Homer still fixated on the tacos he can't have
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Homer's single-minded food obsession

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why does the bank have to be where the taco place was? I don't want your stupid bank! I want tacos!

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: Sir, this is a financial institution.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. Can you at least make it a taco?

jokes.text: "Ook, ook." Are you folks ready to go ape? - Mom? - A professional in an ape mask is still a professional.
jokes.setup: Marge emphasizes trusting money to professionals
jokes.punchline: Bank employee in literal ape costume, Marge still defends professionalism
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual absurdity with Marge's stubborn faith in institutional authority despite obvious ridiculousness

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You should trust your money to professionals.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Employee
dialogue.line: That's right, ma'am.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: They know what they're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Employee
dialogue.line: Absolutely. Now, if you'll excuse me, I need to go bang on this tire with a stick.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: See? Very professional.

jokes.text: Really? Wow! I should have started a long time ago.
jokes.setup: Bank employee's obviously false claim about savings being thrilling
jokes.punchline: Bart's earnest agreement with absurd statement
jokes.explanation: Bart's gullibility played for ironic effect - kid believes obvious sales pitch

dialogue.speaker: Bank Employee
dialogue.line: Wow, saving money is thrilling!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, man, I'm on the edge of my seat.

jokes.text: I got their new Thrifty Saver savings account. 2.3% annual interest instead of the normal 2.25. So a year from now I'll have an extra nickel.
jokes.setup: Lisa excited about better savings account
jokes.punchline: Mathematically works out to trivial nickel difference
jokes.explanation: Smart observational humor about bank marketing vs. mathematical reality, perfect for Lisa's character

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: With compound interest, my savings will grow exponentially!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's great, sweetie. How much extra will you have?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, after calculating the interest rate over five years... five cents.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Five cents? That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, when you account for inflation, it's more like a nickel.

jokes.text: I got the account where you get free customized checks. I chose the Hindenburg flip-book series. Cool!
jokes.setup: Bart excited about free check design
jokes.punchline: Chose historical disaster as flip-book entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - turning historical tragedy into casual entertainment, very Simpsons sensibility

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Check out these free check designs! I'm gonna pick the one with the historical disaster so I can flip through it like a flip-book!

jokes.text: One cent, and no cents.
jokes.setup: Bart writing check amount
jokes.punchline: Formal check language applied to one penny
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay humor from formal/informal language contrast

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Pay to the order of... one penny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: One penny and zero cents.

jokes.text: A million dollars? Thanks, Bart. I owe you one. That's a post-dated check, remember. Don't cash it till the year 10,000. Okay.
jokes.setup: Lisa sees million dollar amount on check
jokes.punchline: Post-dated 8,000 years in future, Lisa casually agrees
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of absurdity with Lisa's matter-of-fact acceptance of impossible timeline

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh my God, a million dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, it's post-dated 8,000 years in the future.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose that's still worth something.

jokes.text: Excellent. As soon as the check clears, I'll let you go.
jokes.setup: Burns holding Lisa captive over the check
jokes.punchline: Treating one penny check like serious financial transaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character moment - treating trivial amount with extreme seriousness

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Why, this check is only for one penny! What kind of father are you, Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Mr. Burns, I...

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: One penny! Do you know what I could buy with one penny? Absolutely nothing! Well, perhaps a gumball, if I'm lucky. But the point is, Simpson, this is an insult to my financial acumen!

jokes.text: Oh, what do you know. Jimbo's real name is Corky.
jokes.setup: Bart looking through autographed checks
jokes.punchline: Tough bully has unexpectedly innocent real name
jokes.explanation: Great character revelation humor - tough guy image undermined by cute name

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, look at this. A check signed by the toughest bully in school.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: His real name is... Butz? Nelson Butz?

jokes.text: Hey! 'Cayman Islands Offshore Holding Corporation'? Krusty was supposed to sign this.
jokes.setup: Bart expecting Krusty's signature
jokes.punchline: Finding evidence of tax evasion instead of an autograph
jokes.explanation: Good plot setup that turns Bart's innocent scheme into the catalyst for the main story.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Krusty, sign this for me!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Sure kid, here ya go.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... 'Offshore accounts in the Cayman Islands'? 'Shell corporations'? This isn't a signature!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Oops, wrong piece of paper!

jokes.text: Oh, crap. I shouldn't have said he was a customer. Oh, crap! I shouldn't have said it was a secret. Oh, crap! I certainly shouldn't have said it was illegal.
jokes.setup: Bank manager trying to maintain confidentiality
jokes.punchline: Accidentally reveals everything through panicked self-correction
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating panic structure - each correction makes it worse, classic comedy writing

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I can't discuss Mr. Simpson's account due to privacy regulations, not that he owes us $50,000, which I definitely shouldn't have mentioned, or that his credit score is in the double digits, forget I said that, or that he tried to open a Swiss account under the name 'Mr. Burns' Illegitimate Son,' please disregard that last part entirely.

jokes.text: It's too hot today.
jokes.setup: Manager's complete information breach
jokes.punchline: Blames weather for his professional failure
jokes.explanation: Nice deflection humor - absurd excuse for professional incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I could blame the weather for this security breach, but that would be irresponsible. Unlike my actual job performance.

jokes.text: What the heck is 'avoision'? - 'Avoision'- It's a crime. Look it up. Would never have- - 'Evasion.' 'Evasion.' I don't say 'evasion,' I say 'avoision.'
jokes.setup: News report about tax evasion
jokes.punchline: Anchor insists on mispronouncing 'evasion' as 'avoision'
jokes.explanation: Memorable wordplay that became part of Simpsons lexicon - stubborn mispronunciation as character trait

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We have breaking news on tax avoision.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: That's right, avoision. Not evasion, avoision.

jokes.text: Yeah. There'll be enough people to do that for me at recess tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Lisa saying Bart shouldn't beat himself up
jokes.punchline: Bart expects physical beating from classmates instead
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor subversion - literal interpretation of figurative expression with realistic consequence

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, you shouldn't beat yourself up about this.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm not worried about that. I'm worried about the physical beating I'll get from the guys at school.

jokes.text: Garnish my celery? Please, Krusty. No jokes. Who's joking?
jokes.setup: Lawyer explains salary garnishment
jokes.punchline: Krusty thinks it means literally garnishing celery
jokes.explanation: Classic Krusty misunderstanding - perfect character moment showing his ignorance of legal/financial terms

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Mr. the Clown, due to the lawsuit, we'll need to garnish your wages.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Garnish my wages? With what, a little parsley?

jokes.text: For example, I sure wish somebody would give me a banana cream pie. Yeah! Uh- I guess we can't afford pies right now. Well, throw something! Oh, that corner!
jokes.setup: Krusty trying to recreate classic pie-in-face gag
jokes.punchline: Budget cuts mean nothing gets thrown, awkward corner reference
jokes.explanation: Good declining-show humor - classic gag fails due to poverty, maintains Krusty's desperate showmanship

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Alright, we're gonna do the classic pie-in-the-face gag!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: But due to budget cuts, I don't have any pie.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So I'm just gonna stand here and pretend to throw it.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: And you just gotta use your imagination... or go sit in that corner over there.

jokes.text: I'll have four Tax Burgers... one 'I.R.S.-wich'- withhold the lettuce- three dependent-size sodas... and a 'FICA-ccino.'
jokes.setup: Ordering at Krusty Burger
jokes.punchline: All menu items renamed with tax/IRS puns
jokes.explanation: Solid pun sequence showing total corporate rebrand around tax theme - 'withhold the lettuce' is particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll have a Burger with... what are these names?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: We renamed everything! The Whopper is now the 'Whopper Deduction,' the fries are 'Fried Returns,' and the shake is a 'Milkshake-down by the IRS.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm being audited, pal! Figured if the government's gonna take my money, at least the customers should understand the pain!

jokes.text: Fill out schedule 'B.' You should receive your burgers in six to eight weeks.
jokes.setup: Fast food order with tax-themed menu
jokes.punchline: Bureaucratic process applied to fast food service
jokes.explanation: Good extension of tax bureau taking over everything - fast food becomes slow bureaucracy

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: I'll have a burger, fries, and a shake.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty Burger Employee
dialogue.line: That'll be $8.95, plus tax.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Tax? On a burger?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty Burger Employee
dialogue.line: Sir, you'll need to fill out Form 1040, get it notarized, and file it in triplicate before we can process your order.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: But I just want to eat!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty Burger Employee
dialogue.line: And I just work here. Next!

jokes.text: Seven hundred dollars.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about gambling losses
jokes.punchline: Marge's matter-of-fact admission of substantial gambling losses
jokes.explanation: Excellent character subversion - wholesome Marge casually admits to significant gambling problem

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, I've lost $47,000 at the slots this month alone.

jokes.text: Forty cents? My grandpa Zev would turn over in his grave... if it wasn't filled with some veteran.
jokes.setup: Krusty upset about low auction price for family heirloom
jokes.punchline: Can't even be buried with dignity due to shared grave
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - classic expression subverted with grim reality of financial ruin affecting even burial

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Can't even be buried with dignity due to shared grave

jokes.text: All I brought is a dime. I didn't know there'd be pornography.
jokes.setup: Pornography lot at auction
jokes.punchline: Ned's innocent disappointment at being outbid
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned character moment - inadvertently reveals interest while maintaining innocent facade

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I was hoping to buy that lot and turn it into a Christian bookstore, but somebody outbid me.

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Sold to the gentleman in the back for $50,000!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Diddly-darn it! I guess the Lord works in mysterious ways.

jokes.text: Oh, my beloved pornography.
jokes.setup: Krusty losing possessions at auction
jokes.punchline: Emotional attachment to pornography collection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's sentimental attachment to inappropriate material

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait! You're auctioning off my entire pornography collection?!

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: That's what the paperwork says, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: But I'm emotionally attached to those magazines! Some of them I've had since I was a teenager!

jokes.text: How much for Krusty's bed? Half a buck. Sold! Good night, everybody. Good night, Moe.
jokes.setup: Selling Krusty's bed
jokes.punchline: Moe immediately going to sleep in the auction house
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe behavior - immediately using his purchase in the most inappropriate setting.

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Lot 47: Krusty the Clown's bed. Do I hear opening bid of $50?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I'll take it!

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: Sold to the man in the audience!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Zzzzzzz...

jokes.text: the I'm-On-A-Rolla Gay
jokes.setup: Krusty's private plane being auctioned
jokes.punchline: Plane named with pun on 'Enola Gay' and Krusty's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Clever historical reference combined with Krusty's brand - perfect character detail with economic wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Auctioneer
dialogue.line: And now, lot 47: a Learjet, formerly owned by Krusty the Clown. It's the Enola Gay... I mean, the 'A-Nola Gay'!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey hey!

jokes.text: One night he looked out the window... and the moon hit his eye like a big pizza pie. We wrote a song about it... but it ended up infringing on one he recorded years before.
jokes.setup: Krusty's story about flying with Dean Martin
jokes.punchline: Accidentally recreated 'That's Amore' without knowing it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor about accidentally recreating famous song - perfect blend of character ignorance and real pop culture

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So I'm flying with Dean Martin, see, and we hit some turbulence. Dean's grabbing the armrests, I'm spilling my drink all over him. The stewardess is trying to calm us down, and Dean starts singing 'That's Amore' to calm his nerves. I join in, we're harmonizing, the whole plane's clapping. It was beautiful!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Turns out, we'd been doing the whole bit by accident. We didn't even know we were recreating the song. Dean says to me, 'Krusty, that's the most beautiful thing I've ever sung with a clown.' I says, 'Dean, the feeling's mutual.'

jokes.text: Nah, I just bought it to soak my feet in.
jokes.setup: Suggestion that Selma's suitcase would go with plane
jokes.punchline: Using leather suitcase as foot bath
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing practical misuse of valuable item - fits Selma's pragmatic character

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: A foot bath? My suitcase?

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose it does have good leather...

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: Ahhhh, that's nice.

jokes.text: No offense, kid, but your mom's a dingbat.
jokes.setup: Bart offers comfort about God closing doors/opening windows
jokes.punchline: Krusty bluntly insults Marge to her son
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty moment - inappropriate honesty due to depression, crushing child's optimism

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You know, when God closes a door, he opens a window.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, and your mother's a real window, if you know what I mean!

jokes.text: I gotta ride the bus like a schnook. I gotta live in an apartment like an idiot. I gotta wait in line with nobodies to buy groceries from a failure!
jokes.setup: Krusty describing his reduced circumstances
jokes.punchline: The escalating series of indignities, ending with insulting the grocery clerk
jokes.explanation: Good escalating structure showing Krusty's elitist worldview crashing into reality.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I used to have a penthouse, a yacht, three ex-wives on the payroll. Now look at me! I'm shopping at a discount grocery store, buying generic cereal, and the cashier just asked me if I had coupons. Coupons! Me! Krusty the Clown! Then she said, 'Sir, you need to step aside, you're blocking the expired dairy section.' Expired dairy! So I said, 'Listen, kid, I've been milking laughs from this city for forty years, so don't you dare talk to me about expired anything!' And she said, 'Whatever, boomer.'

jokes.text: Everywhere I go I see teachers in Ferraris- research scientists drinking champagne.
jokes.setup: Krusty complaining about lack of respect for celebrities
jokes.punchline: Imagines underpaid professionals living lavishly
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of celebrity self-pity - complete disconnect from reality of public service professions

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You know, it's tough being a celebrity. Nobody respects us anymore. We work hard, we entertain people, and what do we get? No respect!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, if I was a plumber or an electrician, I'd be living in a mansion with a private jet and a yacht. But me? I'm struggling to make ends meet on a measly few million a year!

jokes.text: I tried to drink a Coke on the bus... and they took away my pass.
jokes.setup: Krusty describing his fall from grace
jokes.punchline: Mundane bus rule violation as symbol of lost status
jokes.explanation: Excellent detail showing complete status reversal - celebrity can't even break minor public transport rules

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I used to be somebody! I had it all - the fame, the fortune, the respect. Now look at me. You know what really gets me? I got a citation on the bus yesterday for not having exact change. ME! Krusty the Clown! I used to own a network!

jokes.text: I must say, I've had a lovely evening, Agnes. I don't suppose I could come in for a cup of- - Seymour! - Mother. Superintendent Chalmers! - Skinner!
jokes.setup: Skinner's romantic moment with his date
jokes.punchline: Awkward interruption by both mother and boss
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner awkwardness - romantic moment destroyed by authority figures

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, this has been quite the evening. Shall we...

dialogue.speaker: Agnes
dialogue.line: SEYMOUR! I need you to come home at once! My stories are on!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Skinner! Why aren't you at home grading papers? There's been a complaint about the cafeteria meatloaf!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Mother? Superintendent Chalmers? What are you both doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Agnes
dialogue.line: Don't you take that tone with me, young man!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Seymour, this is highly irregular.

jokes.text: What I wouldn't give for something... to interrupt this awkward moment.
jokes.setup: Skinner trapped in uncomfortable social situation
jokes.punchline: Explicitly wishes for interruption that's about to happen
jokes.explanation: Good meta setup for incoming plane crash - character literally asking for what's about to happen

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: I wish something would happen right now to get me out of this awkward situation.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: SKINNER!

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Krusty's shoes! Okay, folks, show's over. Nothing to see here. Show's- Oh, my God! A horrible plane crash! Hey, everybody, get a load of this flaming wreckage. Come on, crowd around.
jokes.setup: Police chief managing crash scene
jokes.punchline: Contradicts himself by encouraging gawking immediately after discouraging it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - complete contradiction of police protocol driven by his own curiosity

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright, break it up, break it up. Nothing to see here, folks. Move along.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, what are you waiting for? Get a good look! This is the best thing that's happened all week!

jokes.text: Don't worry, son. I'm sure he's up in heaven right now laughing it up with all the other celebrities- John Dillinger, Ty Cobb, Joseph Stalin.
jokes.setup: Homer comforting Bart about Krusty's death
jokes.punchline: His list of 'celebrities' in heaven includes a gangster and dictator
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor showing Homer's confused moral universe where fame equals goodness.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, son, Krusty's in a better place now. He's up in heaven with all the other great comedians.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really, Dad? Who else is up there?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, you know... Lucille Ball, Bob Hope, and... uh... Al Capone and Mussolini.

jokes.text: I'm sure he's up in heaven right now... laughing it up with all the other celebrities- John Dillinger, Ty Cobb, Joseph Stalin.
jokes.setup: Homer comforting Bart about Krusty's death
jokes.punchline: Lists criminals and dictators as heavenly celebrities
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - Homer's complete moral confusion about who belongs in heaven

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There, there, son. Krusty's in a better place now. He's up in heaven with all the other greats. Lincoln, Kennedy, Babe Ruth...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, those guys aren't in heaven.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, that's right. Well, he's down there with Hitler, Stalin, and Bud Fox.

jokes.text: You might remember me from such show business funerals... as 'Andre the Giant, We Hardly Knew Ye'... and 'Shemp Howard: Today We Mourn a Stooge.'
jokes.setup: Troy McClure introducing himself at funeral
jokes.punchline: Signature introduction format applied to morbid specialty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Troy McClure moment - signature formula applied to inappropriate context, great fake titles

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Gladiator' and 'Escape from the Planet of the Apes.' But I'm here today as a professional mourner. You see, I've made a speciality out of crying at funerals. I even cried at my own mother's funeral... and I hardly knew her.

jokes.text: your elevated blood-alcohol level... probably helped you burn up quicker.
jokes.setup: Sideshow Mel's eulogy for Krusty
jokes.punchline: Finding positive spin on alcoholism in death
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate eulogy - finding silver lining in addiction and horrible death

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Mel
dialogue.line: Krusty was a man of many vices. Alcohol, gambling, and loose women consumed him. But in death, we can take solace in knowing that his liver finally gets a break.

jokes.text: You must be over 18 for the 10:00. It gets a little blue.
jokes.setup: Announcing shivah schedule
jokes.punchline: Adult-only mourning session with inappropriate content
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of religious mourning ritual with comedy club rules - perfectly inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such funeral films as 'Permanent Waving: A Life Cut Short' and 'The Mane Event.' I'm here to announce the shivah schedule.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: We'll have the regular family mourning from 2 to 5 PM. But for those of you who prefer a more... adult atmosphere, we're hosting an adults-only mourning session from 8 PM onwards, featuring off-color jokes, crude humor, and stories better left untold.

jokes.text: By a nearly two-to-one vote, the smiling Krusty was chosen.
jokes.setup: Post office stamp design vote between happy Krusty and death scene
jokes.punchline: Significant minority wanted the death stamp
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about public morbid fascination - substantial minority wanted death imagery on stamps

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: In a surprising turn of events, a significant minority of voters wanted the death stamp.

jokes.text: Why, you could wake up dead tomorrow. Well, good night.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Bart about death
jokes.punchline: Immediately threatens him with death before bedtime
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting - completely counterproductive comfort ending with casual death threat

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now son, death is a natural part of life. We all go eventually. But don't worry, it's nothing to be scared of.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really, Dad? That actually helps.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Of course it does. Now, you better get to bed, or I'll kill you.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just saying, get to bed or you're dead meat.

jokes.text: - Yes, dear, in your mind. - No, on the street. - On the street in your mind.
jokes.setup: Bart insisting he saw living Krusty
jokes.punchline: Marge's determination to rationalize away reality
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of maternal dismissal - Marge's stubborn refusal to consider impossible truth

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, you must have imagined it. Krusty died three years ago.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm telling you, I saw him at the Kwik-E-Mart!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That was probably just someone who looked like him. The mind plays tricks, especially on children.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It WAS Krusty! He talked to me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, perhaps it was a very lifelike cardboard cutout. Or maybe you were having a vivid daydream. Your father does that all the time.

jokes.text: I went through the same thing when Lyndon Johnson died.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining grief hallucinations
jokes.punchline: Personal attachment to random political figure
jokes.explanation: Excellent character detail - Marge's unexpected political crush revealed through grief comparison

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Sometimes when you lose someone, your mind plays tricks on you. You see them everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Like when I thought I saw Elvis at the Kwik-E-Mart?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Exactly. It's grief, Homer. Your subconscious is trying to process the loss.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So that's why I've been crying every time I see a photo of Gerald Ford?

jokes.text: telling you... would violate the patient-doctor privilege... just as if I were to tell you that Jasper here has five seconds to live. - What'd he say? - He said I'm next.
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert explaining confidentiality
jokes.punchline: Immediately violates it while explaining it, leads to misunderstanding about death
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural irony - violates confidentiality while explaining it, plus misheard death sentence

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Now, what you tell me is strictly confidential. I don't discuss my patients with anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, except Jasper, but he's practically family. Say, Jasper, remember that time Old Man Jenkins came in here thinking he had only six months to live?

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: Hehehehe, yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Old Man
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Six months?!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Oh no, no, no—that was years ago. You're fine now!

dialogue.speaker: Old Man
dialogue.line: Years?! I'm still here!

jokes.text: Arr! That's Handsome Pete. He dances for nickels. Pete, ye got some customers.
jokes.setup: Searching for Krusty on the docks
jokes.punchline: Misidentified as dancing performer, presumably awful
jokes.explanation: Great visual setup for pathetic street performer - 'Handsome Pete' doing degrading work

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ahoy! Have ye seen Krusty the Clown? He was supposed to meet me here on the docks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Krusty? I think I saw him over there doing some kind of... dance?

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Dance? Shiver me timbers, that scallywag owes me money! I'll have a word with him!

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Hold on... that ain't Krusty. That's just some fella flailin' about like a fish out of water. Worst dancin' I ever seen!

jokes.text: Not a quarter! Arr! He'll be dancing for hours.
jokes.setup: Someone presumably gives Pete money
jokes.punchline: Wrong denomination means extended performance
jokes.explanation: Good callback extending the pathetic performer gag - trapped by overpayment

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ahoy! A twenty dollar bill! That be the going rate for me sea shanty.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... this be a one dollar bill! Arrr, ye cheap landlubber!

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Now I'll have to sing the extended version!

jokes.text: Not a quarter! Arr! He'll be dancing for hours.
jokes.setup: Handsome Pete being paid to dance
jokes.punchline: Overpayment means extended suffering for the performer
jokes.explanation: Callback that shows the dark side of the street performance economy.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Arrr, that scurvy dog Handsome Pete be gettin' paid handsomely to dance! But blow me down, if we don't overpay the bilge rat, he'll have to suffer through more of his own performin'!

jokes.text: And Krusty's superfluous third nipple.
jokes.setup: Lisa identifying Krusty through distinctive features
jokes.punchline: Bizarre anatomical detail as identifying characteristic
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully weird character detail - extra nipple as plot device and absurd identifier

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's Krusty! I'd recognize that liver spot shaped like New Jersey anywhere.

jokes.text: Or kill us.
jokes.setup: Discussion of what Krusty should do now that they know
jokes.punchline: Bart casually suggests murder as option
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor understatement - child matter-of-factly suggests being murdered

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why don't we just kill him?

jokes.text: The incessant beep of the global positioning system... is all the companionship I need. Tell me where you are now, you bastard!
jokes.setup: Krusty claiming he doesn't need human company
jokes.punchline: Immediately frustrated with GPS technology
jokes.explanation: Good character contradiction - claims technology suffices then immediately argues with it

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Who needs human company? I got my GPS!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Turn left ahead. Turn left ahead. Why you breaking my chops?

jokes.text: Tell me where you are now, you bastard! All right, I admit it. I miss the phonies.
jokes.setup: Krusty claiming to be happy with his simple life
jokes.punchline: His rage at technology followed by admission of loneliness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's facade cracking under isolation.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm happy with my simple life. No fancy gadgets, no computers, just me and my cigar.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Who needs the internet?! Who needs email?! It's all garbage! Garbage!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: But... nobody calls me anymore.

jokes.text: What about being an illiterate TV clown who's still more respected than all the scientists, doctors and educators in the country put together?
jokes.setup: Bart trying to convince Krusty to return to showbiz
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the absurd cultural priorities that favor entertainers
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary delivered through a child's innocent observation about cultural values.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Krusty, you gotta come back to showbiz. People miss you!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Nah, kid. I'm done. Show business chewed me up and spit me out.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Krusty, you're a legend! You could do anything you want.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Anything? Kid, I can't even get a job at the car wash. But some guy who can juggle gets his own talk show.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's crazy!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Welcome to America, boy. Where a washed-up clown means nothing, but a guy who can do backflips is basically royalty.

jokes.text: The life of Rory B. Bellows is insured... for a surprisingly large amount.
jokes.setup: Question about tax problems
jokes.punchline: Plans to fake death again for insurance money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty ending - learned nothing, ready to commit more fraud using established fake identity

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Tax problems? Hey, I've got a solution - I'll fake my death again and collect the insurance money!

jokes.text: Shh!
jokes.setup: End credits or final scene
jokes.punchline: Mysterious shushing sound
jokes.explanation: Minimal impact ending sound effect - likely visual gag context missing from transcript

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Mysterious shushing sound

tmdb_overview: In a scheme to get Krusty's autograph, Bart sends him a check. When Krusty's offshore holding corporation endorses the check, his illegal tax shelter is exposed. In despair after the government auctions off his possessions, Krusty fakes his own death.
insight: Bart's tax evasion scheme delivers 58 jokes at 2.83-per-minute pace, anchored by dark subversion.
